planting.note: Plant in spring near a large support it can cover.

directions: Give this rampant rambler a big tree or pergola and plant in well-drained soil. Once settled it asks only for the removal of dead wood. Great trusses of single flowers open yellowish and fade to white, filling the air with fragrance in one summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring only where enormous size is welcome.

directions: This is one of the largest of all roses, so plant it to engulf a mature tree, never a small trellis. It needs strong support and essentially no pruning once established. Vast foaming sprays of small, single white flowers scent the garden in midsummer, followed by tiny hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a large tree or building to climb.

directions: Plant this powerful rambler where its long canes can reach into a tree or across a big pergola. Give strong support and prune only to remove dead wood. Immense clusters of small, semi-double creamy-white flowers open in summer with a sweet, fruity scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large arch, tree, or pergola.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and give this vigorous rambler a sturdy structure to cover. Thin old canes after flowering to keep it healthy. Large trusses of small, semi-double white flowers with golden stamens perfume the garden in a single summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light shade to keep the violet color from fading.

directions: Plant this nearly thornless rambler where a little afternoon shade will protect the unusual coloring. Remove old wood after bloom and tie in new canes. Clusters of small semi-double flowers open violet-purple, streaked white, and fade toward slate-blue.

planting.note: Plant in spring in an open, airy spot to limit mildew.

directions: Plant into good soil and provide an arch or fence for the flexible canes. Cut out spent flowered wood each year and tie in fresh growth for air circulation. Dense clusters of small, double crimson-red flowers create a bold single midsummer display.

planting.note: Plant in spring on an arch or pergola where blooms can hang.

directions: Plant this graceful, semi-evergreen rambler where its trailing sprays can dangle from a support. Prune lightly after flowering to remove old wood. Dainty clusters of small blush-pink to cream flowers hang like garlands in a single early-summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates poorer soil and some shade.

directions: Plant this tough, glossy-leaved rambler even in a difficult or shadier spot and give it a wall or fence. Prune only to shape after the main flush. Yellow buds open to creamy-white flowers with a light apple scent in early summer, with an occasional later bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring on an arch or pillar; nearly thornless.

directions: Plant this manageable, almost thornless rambler in well-drained soil and train it to a support. Deadhead lightly to encourage a repeat show. Apricot-yellow buds open to soft cream flushed pink and, unusually for a rambler, it reblooms later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring on an arch or fence; blooms late in the season.

directions: Plant this flexible-caned rambler where it can drape over a support and set it in well-drained soil. Cut out old flowered wood after bloom. Cascading sprays of small, double pure-white flowers with a sweet scent open later than most ramblers.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall in milder zones.

directions: Plant this vigorous, thorny climber in a warm, sheltered spot with good drainage and handle the canes carefully. Prune sparingly, as it dislikes hard cutting. Large single, pale-yellow flowers with amber stamens open over a very long season into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm climate with room to sprawl.

directions: Give this evergreen climbing species a large fence or bank and plant in well-drained soil; seed needs cold stratification but plants are usually set from nursery stock. Once established it is drought-tolerant and needs only light shaping. Large, single fragrant white flowers with golden centers open in spring in a single flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a pillar or wall; one of the few repeat-flowering ramblers.

directions: Plant this restrained, nearly thornless climber in well-drained soil and tie in the canes. Deadhead regularly to sustain its long flowering. Small, loosely double blooms in a blend of soft yellow, pink, and cream repeat from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a strong arch, pillar, or tree.

directions: Plant this vigorous old rambler in well-drained soil and give it a sturdy support to climb. Prune lightly after flowering to remove spent wood. Large upright trusses of small semi-double flowers open blush and fade to creamy white with a strong orange scent, followed by small hips.

planting.note: Set out balled or container plants in spring or early fall into cool, acidic soil.

directions: Plant with the top of the rootball slightly above grade in humus-rich, acidic soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Avoid deep planting and never let the roots dry out or sit in standing water. Expect broad clusters of lilac-purple bells in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist acidic soil; it can spread aggressively.

directions: Give it acidic, humus-rich soil and shelter from drying wind. Mulch generously and prune after flowering to keep the dense mound in bounds, since it can naturalize freely. Mauve-purple trusses cover the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Plant this tidy dome shallowly in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Its felted silvery new growth and dense habit need little pruning. Buds open apple-blossom pink and fade to white in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, lightly shaded spot with sharp drainage.

directions: Choose a sheltered site with acidic, well-drained soil, as late frosts can nip the early growth. The rounded bronze-tinted young leaves are a feature in their own right. Nodding pink bell flowers appear in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a mild, sheltered garden with acidic soil.

directions: Give this tree-like species a sheltered position in deep, acidic, moisture-retentive soil, protected from hard frost. Mulch well and water during dry summers while it establishes. Bold red to pink flower trusses open from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light woodland shade with acidic soil.

directions: Site in dappled shade with acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and shelter from cold wind. Mulch the shallow roots and avoid deep cultivation nearby. Prized for unusually blue-lavender flowers that smother the shrub in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic woodland soil.

directions: Plant shallowly in cool, acidic, humus-rich soil with light shade and shelter. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist. Large, fragrant, soft-pink trusses open in mid to late spring on this parent of many hybrids.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: This adaptable native takes acidic, well-drained soil in sun to part shade with a cool, mulched root run. Water during drought while establishing. Rose-pink flowers with speckled throats appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, acidic soil in a rock garden or trough.

directions: Give this alpine dwarf sharp drainage in gritty, acidic soil, ideal for rock gardens and troughs. Keep it out of hot afternoon sun and never let it bake dry. Tiny grey-green leaves are covered with violet-blue flowers in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil with light shade and a cool mulch over the roots. Extremely cold hardy, though early blooms can be caught by late frost. Bright rosy-purple flowers open very early, sometimes in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; site out of frost pockets to protect early flowers.

directions: Set in acidic, well-drained soil with a mulched root zone, avoiding frost pockets since it flowers very early. Prune lightly after bloom to shape. Rosy-purple flowers appear on bare stems before the leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, moist soil; tolerates southern heat well.

directions: This heat-tolerant Southern native likes moist, acidic soil and part shade with a cool mulch. Water while establishing and prune after flowering if needed. Fragrant golden-yellow to orange flowers open in early to mid spring, often before leaf-out.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic woodland soil.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, acidic soil at the woodland edge where it can sucker into a loose colony. Keep the shallow roots mulched and watered. Fragrant pale-pink to white flowers open with or just before the leaves in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic soil; shelter early blooms from frost.

directions: Plant in cool, acidic, well-drained soil in light shade, avoiding frost pockets that damage the early flowers. Mulch the roots and water in dry spells. Large soft-pink blooms open with the whorled leaves in mid spring, and foliage colors well in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Grow in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in sun to part shade with a cool mulch. Water during dry weather and prune lightly after bloom. Intensely fragrant yellow flowers open in late spring, and the foliage turns rich red and purple in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: This tough, cold-hardy hybrid tolerates more sun than most rhododendrons in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch. Little pruning is needed on the dense mound. Bright lavender-pink flowers appear in early spring, and the small leaves turn mahogany in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A dependable, cold-hardy hybrid for acidic, humus-rich soil with a cool, mulched root zone in part shade. Deadhead spent trusses and water in dry spells. Deep red flowers with dark blotches open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: An adaptable, vigorous catawba hybrid that tolerates heat, cold, and some sun in acidic, well-drained soil. Mulch the roots and water while establishing. Lavender-pink trusses cover the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: One of the toughest hybrids for temperature extremes, it takes acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Deadhead after flowering and water in drought. Rosy-lilac trusses open in late spring on this reliable landscape shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerates slightly less acidic soil than most.

directions: A dense, reliable white hybrid that tolerates a wider soil pH range and is often used as an understock. Give it well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. White flowers with faint yellow markings open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: This hardy, rounded hybrid takes acidic, well-drained soil in part shade with a cool mulch and needs little pruning. Water during dry weather. Rounded snowball trusses of white flowers open in mid to late spring, the name meaning ball of snow.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Deadhead spent flowers and shelter from harsh wind. Deep royal-purple flowers with near-black ruffled blotches open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A well-loved Dexter hybrid with handsome glossy foliage, best in acidic, humus-rich soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Water in dry spells while establishing. Pink trusses with bronze-gold throats open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A large, vigorous hybrid for acidic, humus-rich soil in part shade with a cool, mulched root run. Give it room to spread and water while establishing. Large deep-pink trusses open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: An old, dependable hybrid that grows into a large dome in acidic, humus-rich soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Deadhead after bloom and water in drought. Large rosy-crimson trusses cover the plant in late spring.

scientific_name: Rhododendron 'The Honourable Jean Marie de Montague'

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A classic red hybrid with sturdy, compact growth, best in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Water while establishing and deadhead spent trusses. Bright scarlet-red flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Deadhead after flowering and shelter from strong wind. Ruffled lavender-blue flowers with purple-blotched throats open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A tough, heat-tolerant old hybrid with dark leathery foliage, thriving in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Water in dry weather. White flowers flushed pale mauve with a yellow-brown flare open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil; good for edging.

directions: This hardy dwarf makes a neat low mound in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch and needs almost no pruning. Its small leaves take on a dusty blue cast. Violet-blue flowers cover the plant in mid spring, good for edging and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: A superior yakushimanum selection with heavy silver-felted new growth, best in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Little pruning is needed on the dense mound. Buds open deep pink and fade to pale pink and white in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A compact yakushimanum hybrid for acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade, needing minimal pruning. Water while establishing. Flowers open peach-pink and cream, softening to near-white as they age in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: A classic Kurume azalea forming a dense, spreading mound in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch. Prune just after bloom to shape. Vivid rosy-red flowers cover the small glossy leaves in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: A widely grown white evergreen azalea for acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Shear lightly after flowering to maintain the rounded form. Pure white funnel-shaped flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A popular Exbury deciduous azalea for moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in sun to part shade with a cool mulch. Water in dry weather while establishing. Flame-orange flowers open from ruffled buds in mid to late spring, and foliage colors in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A bright Exbury deciduous azalea for moist, acidic, well-drained soil in sun to part shade with a cool mulch. Water during dry summers. Glowing golden-orange flowers with a warm bronze tint open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: A low, spreading Gable evergreen azalea for acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Shear lightly after bloom to shape. Double rose-pink flowers that resemble tiny roses open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: A hardy Gable evergreen azalea for acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulch in part shade. Prune after flowering if needed. Bright brick-red flowers open in mid spring, and the foliage turns wine-red in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: An Encore-series reblooming evergreen azalea that flowers in spring and again in late summer through fall, given acidic, well-drained soil and more sun than old azaleas. Keep it mulched and watered. Purple flowers appear across multiple seasons.

planting.note: A compact dwarf hybrid for small gardens; plant spring or fall.

directions: Set into sharply drained acidic soil and mulch lightly over the fine roots. This tidy little shrub rarely needs pruning. Deep violet-purple flowers appear in early spring against tiny aromatic leaves.

planting.note: A strongly scented yellow azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade and mulch to keep roots cool. The foliage turns rich red and orange in autumn. Powerfully fragrant golden-yellow flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: An alpine dwarf for rock gardens; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in gritty, sharply drained acidic soil where its cushion form gets good air movement. The tiny scaly leaves have a blue-green cast. Violet-blue flowers cover the mound in mid spring.

planting.note: Grown for rare blue tones; plant spring or fall in light shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled shade sheltered from wind. Cooler growing conditions bring out the deepest blue flower color. Lavender-blue to violet blooms open in mid spring.

planting.note: A cold-hardy Girard hybrid; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch over the fine surface roots. The glossy foliage often takes on a reddish winter tint. Bright rose-pink flowers cover the plant in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A dwarf late-blooming Satsuki azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant this low, spreading azalea in acidic, well-drained soil at the front of a bed. Its dense mat suits edging and foundation plantings. Large ruffled pink flowers open in late spring, later than most azaleas.

planting.note: A Rutherfordiana-type azalea for mild climates; plant fall or spring.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in filtered light and mulch to keep roots cool. Its upright habit suits containers and warm-climate borders. Ruffled deep-pink flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: A Knap Hill azalea with ball-shaped trusses; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich soil in light shade and mulch to conserve moisture. Its rounded trusses stand out among looser azalea forms. Rose-pink semi-double flowers form tight balls in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A double-flowered deciduous azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and mulch the roots. Give it filtered light for the best flower color. Double blooms in soft pink, cream, and apricot tones open in late spring.

planting.note: A very cold-hardy Northern Lights azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in a cold-winter garden and mulch the roots. This University of Minnesota hybrid shrugs off deep cold. Fragrant golden-yellow flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A cold-hardy Northern Lights azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot and mulch to protect the roots through hard winters. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead wood. Fragrant deep rose-pink flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A cold-hardy Northern Lights azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled sun and mulch the shallow roots. Bred for extreme hardiness, it flowers reliably after cold winters. Bright orange flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A mildew-resistant Northern Lights azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Its foliage resists powdery mildew better than many deciduous azaleas. Creamy white flowers with a yellow blotch open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A reblooming Encore azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil where it gets several hours of sun to fuel repeat bloom. Give it more light than old-style azaleas and keep it evenly watered. Purple flowers appear in spring and flush again in summer and fall.

planting.note: A reblooming Encore azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in a spot with morning sun and light afternoon shade for repeat flowering. Water consistently through summer to support the second flush. White flowers streaked and flecked with purple open in spring, summer, and fall.

planting.note: A reblooming Encore azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil with several hours of sun to encourage rebloom. Mulch and keep evenly moist, especially during summer flowering. Large purple flowers open in spring and return in summer and fall.

planting.note: A hardy semi-evergreen Japanese azalea; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade and mulch over the roots. It holds some leaves in mild winters and drops them where cold. Salmon to orange-red flowers open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: The classic late-blooming Satsuki species; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in filtered light and keep it evenly moist. Its low, spreading habit makes it a favorite for bonsai and edging. Large single flowers, often red or pink, open in late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in fall so roots settle before the winter bloom season.

directions: Use acidic, well-drained soil and keep the root ball slightly proud of the surface. Provide part shade to protect the crisp white petals from sun scorch. The full, formal-double white flowers open from mid to late season.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sheltered part-shade bed.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with good drainage and keep evenly moist during dry weather. The formal-double blooms open white and blush to orchid-pink toward the petal edges. Flowers appear in mid to late winter and last well when protected from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in part shade with excellent drainage.

directions: Set into acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. The large semi-double to peony-form blooms are a deep black-red with showy stamens. Expect a compact, upright habit and midseason-to-late flowering.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade; this is a tough, cold-tolerant old cultivar.

directions: Plant high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch generously. The full peony-form dark-red flowers show a dense boss of curled petaloids. Its vigorous, upright growth and midseason bloom make it a reliable landscape choice.

planting.note: Plant in fall; the low, spreading form suits a border edge or espalier.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with good drainage and leave room for the wide, arching branches. Large semi-double deep-pink flowers open early, so choose a spot sheltered from frost. Also sold under the Japanese name Akashigata.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in dappled shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. The semi-double blush-pink flowers have narrow, magnolia-like petals and open in mid season. Its neat, upright habit needs little pruning; also known as Hagoromo.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade to preserve the striped petals.

directions: Plant in acidic soil, keeping the root ball high and mulched. The semi-double white-to-pink flowers are streaked and splashed with carmine, and no two blooms match. Expect midseason flowering on a bushy, upright shrub.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in part shade with good drainage.

directions: Set high in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This cultivar is prized for very large semi-double brilliant-red blooms with bold stamens. Flowering comes midseason on a vigorous, upright plant.

planting.note: Plant in fall and allow room for its large, open habit.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the surface roots. The very large semi-double salmon-rose flowers hang gracefully from a vigorous, somewhat pendulous plant. Expect midseason bloom and give it space to arch.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this is one of the more cold- and heat-tolerant japonicas.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with reliable drainage and mulch well. The semi-double light-pink flowers show darker undersides and open over a long midseason period. Its hardiness and vigor make it a dependable garden shrub.

planting.note: Plant in fall; the low, spreading form works along a wall.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and leave room for the wide, low habit. The large anemone-form flowers are a soft light pink with a full center of petaloids. Bloom comes early to midseason, so shelter it from frost pockets.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in part shade with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant high in acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots moist and cool. Often called a benchmark camellia, it bears very large semi-double coral-red blooms with fluted petals. Flowering runs midseason on a vigorous, upright shrub.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade.

directions: Set into acidic, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist during summer. The formal-double flowers are pale pink striped and flecked with rose-red. Expect midseason bloom on an upright, bushy plant that varies from flower to flower.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. The semi-double pink flowers are edged with white and streaked with deeper rose. This long-cultivated variety flowers midseason and is also sold as Hikarugenji.

planting.note: Plant in fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the surface roots. The peony-form flowers are a rich ruby-rose that catches the low autumn light. Blooms open early in the sasanqua season on a spreading, vigorous plant.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; its low, dense form suits foundations and low hedges.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This compact, low-spreading shrub carries semi-double rose-red flowers over a long fall-to-winter season. Its neat habit needs little pruning and works well in containers.

planting.note: Plant in fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with reliable drainage and mulch well. The large semi-double white flowers are edged in pink and open in fall. Its upright, vigorous frame is well suited to training on a wall or growing as a tall screen.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; the low, dense habit suits mass planting.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to hold moisture. The peony-form deep-red flowers appear over a long fall season on a compact, spreading plant. Its dense habit makes a good low informal hedge or large container specimen.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with good drainage and mulch to keep roots cool. The single flowers open white flushed with pink at the petal edges, much like apple blossom. Bloom comes in fall on an upright, spreading shrub.

planting.note: Plant in fall; the low, arching form suits banks and low walls.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. The formal-double bright rose-pink flowers appear over a long fall season. Its low, spreading branches cascade nicely over a wall or bank.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots. The full double soft-pink flowers show a ruffled center and glossy foliage sets them off well. Bloom comes in fall on a dense, upright plant.

planting.note: Plant in fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots moist. The semi-double crimson flowers glow against dark evergreen leaves in the fall garden. Its upright, bushy habit suits hedging and foundation plantings.

planting.note: Plant in fall; this cold-hardy selection extends camellias into colder gardens.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch heavily to protect roots through cold winters. Part of the cold-hardy April series, it bears anemone-form red flowers in spring. Site it out of drying winter wind for the best flower display.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sheltered part-shade spot in colder zones.

directions: Plant high in acidic soil and mulch well to buffer winter cold. This cold-hardy April series selection produces semi-double shell-pink flowers in spring. Shelter from winter wind and morning sun to keep the buds and blooms intact.

planting.note: Plant in fall; grown from a cold-hardy Korean seed source.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to shield roots from hard freezes. Selected for winter hardiness, it opens single fiery-red flowers with golden stamens in late winter to spring. Give it a protected site to reduce cold damage to the buds.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; its narrow, columnar form fits tight spaces.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This markedly upright, narrow sasanqua carries single lavender-pink flowers in fall. Its slim habit makes a fine vertical accent or narrow screen along a fence.

planting.note: Plant in fall; this low, weeping form suits containers and hanging displays.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil or a large container and mulch to hold moisture. This trailing, weeping sasanqua drapes its branches and carries peony-form pink flowers in fall. Let it cascade over a wall, bank, or large pot for the best effect.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the shallow roots. The semi-double flowers are white shading to lavender-pink, with petals arranged in an overlapping star pattern. Bloom comes in fall on an upright, slightly open plant.

planting.note: Plant in fall; this cold-hardy hybrid extends the season into colder zones.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to protect roots from freezing. A cold-hardy Ackerman hybrid, it opens single reddish-lavender flowers with a light fragrance in late fall. Shelter from cold wind to preserve the blooms.

planting.note: Plant in fall; among the earliest and hardiest of the fall bloomers.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool and protected. This cold-hardy hybrid produces anemone-form white flowers very early in the autumn season. Its spreading habit works as a low screen or informal hedge in colder climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot with room for its arching form.

directions: Give this large shrub open ground so its fountain-like branches can arch to the ground. Prune right after flowering by removing the oldest canes at the base, since blooms form on old wood. Clusters of white flowers cascade along the stems in late spring.

planting.note: Site in full sun with space for its tall, arching habit.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil and avoid heavy shade for best flowering. Thin out the oldest stems just after bloom to keep the shrub open and vigorous. Double white button flowers line the bare branches in early spring, followed by orange fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the earliest spirea bloom.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and water through the first season to settle the fine roots. Prune after flowering to shape the wiry, twiggy branches. Small white flowers wreath the stems very early in spring before the narrow leaves emerge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest flower color.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and mulch lightly to hold moisture. Cut the plant back hard in late winter and deadhead spent heads to prompt rebloom. Rosy-pink flat flower clusters open across summer above blue-green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the foliage tones.

directions: Grow in full sun so the new growth shows its coppery-orange color before turning yellow-green. Shear after the first bloom to spark fresh colorful foliage and more flowers. Pink flower clusters appear in early summer over golden leaves that redden in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest gold leaves.

directions: Place in full sun to keep the foliage a clear golden-yellow rather than green. Trim lightly after bloom and shear in late winter to keep the mound tidy. Pink flower heads rise above chartreuse-gold leaves in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; ideal for the front of a border.

directions: Set this compact shrub in well-drained soil and water until roots take hold. Deadhead faded blooms to extend flowering and shear in late winter to renew. Rose-pink flower clusters cover the neat green mound through much of summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to enjoy its mixed flower colors.

directions: Grow in full sun and average soil, keeping it evenly moist during dry spells. Deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming through the season. White, pink, and rose flowers appear together on the same plant in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for vivid new-growth color.

directions: Site in full sun so the red new tips and gold leaves stay brightly colored. Shear lightly after bloom to keep the low mound dense and encourage rebloom. Pink flower clusters top the foliage in early summer over red-tipped golden leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest flower color.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and mulch to conserve moisture. Cut back hard in late winter and deadhead through summer to keep it blooming. Bright rosy-red flower heads stand above green foliage that turns burgundy in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for bold foliage and bloom.

directions: Grow in full sun so the orange spring growth and gold summer leaves color well. Trim after the first flush to prompt a second round of flowers. Large pink flower heads open in summer over foliage that shifts from orange to gold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for compact golden foliage.

directions: Place in full sun to keep the small leaves golden through the season. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain the dense, dwarf habit. Rich pink flowers contrast against gold foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for rich purple flower color.

directions: Set in full sun and well-drained soil, watering during establishment. Deadhead spent blooms and shear in late winter to keep the plant tidy. Deep purple-pink flower clusters open in summer above dark-tinged green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for fiery seasonal foliage.

directions: Grow in full sun so the orange-red new growth and fall color develop fully. Shear after bloom to renew the foliage and encourage reflowering. Pink flowers appear in summer over leaves that move from orange to fiery red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a low golden cushion.

directions: Site in full sun to keep the dwarf mound bright gold with bronze new tips. Trim lightly after flowering to maintain the compact form. Pink flower heads dot the golden foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun as a low groundcover shrub.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil where its low, spreading habit can knit together as a cover. Shear lightly after bloom to keep the fine-textured mound dense. Soft pink flower clusters cover the tiny blue-green leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerant and easy to grow.

directions: Set in full sun in average, well-drained soil and water during dry spells. Cut back hard in late winter to encourage vigorous flowering stems. Deep pink flower clusters open in summer above foliage with reddish new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to part shade for strong fall color.

directions: Grow in full sun for the best flowering and autumn foliage display. Prune lightly in late winter to shape the compact, rounded mound. White flat flower clusters open in late spring over birch-like leaves that turn red-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in average garden soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water regularly the first year to establish. Shear off faded flower heads and cut back in late winter to renew growth. Flat pink flower clusters bloom across summer on this compact hybrid.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room for arching stems.

directions: Give this shrub open space so its slender branches can arch outward. Prune out old canes right after bloom since flowers form on the previous year's wood. Masses of small white flowers wreath the stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist to wet ground.

directions: Site in consistently moist soil, ideal for pond edges and rain gardens. Cut back suckering stems as needed to control its spreading thicket habit. Spiky rose-pink flower plumes rise above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist native-plant settings.

directions: Plant in damp meadows or streamside soil where it can spread naturally. Little pruning is needed beyond removing old stems in late winter. Slender white flower spires bloom in summer and draw many pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist, acidic ground.

directions: Grow in wet meadows and bog edges where the soil stays damp. Allow it to form loose colonies, cutting back old woody stems in late winter. Fuzzy rose-pink flower spires stand upright in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for textured, curly foliage.

directions: Set in full sun where the deeply cut, crinkled leaves show off their texture. Deadhead and shear lightly after bloom to keep it compact and reblooming. Rosy-pink flower clusters open in summer over the twisted foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for soft yellow foliage.

directions: Grow in full sun so the pale butter-yellow leaves keep their gentle glow. Shear after the first bloom to freshen foliage and prompt rebloom. Pink flower heads rise above the light golden mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a long, seedless bloom.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; this sterile hybrid reblooms without deadheading. Trim lightly to shape but heavy pruning is not required for continued flowering. Purple-red flower heads appear from late spring into fall over red-tipped foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; very cold hardy.

directions: Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil; it tolerates harsh winters well. Prune out old stems after flowering to keep the shrub open. Clusters of white flowers open along the branches in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Site with room for its arching, suckering habit and remove old canes after bloom. It tolerates a range of soils as long as drainage is good. Rounded white flower clusters cover the zig-zag branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for gold foliage on a dwarf plant.

directions: Grow in full sun to keep the leaves golden and bronze-tinged in spring. Shear after the first bloom to renew color and encourage more flowers. Pink flower clusters open over golden foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for lime-gold foliage.

directions: Place in full sun so the foliage holds its bright lime-yellow color. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the mound rounded and dense. Pink flower heads appear in summer over leaves that redden in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for white bloom on gold foliage.

directions: Grow in full sun to bring out the golden leaf color against white flowers. Shear after bloom to freshen foliage and shape the plant. Flat white flower clusters open in early summer over the gold mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for deep red flowers.

directions: Set in full sun and well-drained soil, watering during establishment. Cut back in late winter and deadhead through summer for repeat bloom. Rich red flower heads stand above green foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room to arch and mound.

directions: Give this shrub open space so its arching branches form a rounded mound. Prune old canes just after flowering since bloom comes on old wood. Dense white flower clusters smother the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for compact golden growth.

directions: Grow in full sun to keep the golden foliage bright and the habit tidy. Shear lightly after bloom to encourage a second flush. Pink flower heads top the yellow foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for early cascading bloom.

directions: Site in full sun with space for the slender arching branches to spread. Prune out old stems right after flowering to keep the shrub graceful. Small white flowers line the wiry branches in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a compact pink-flowered shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water during establishment. Deadhead and shear in late winter to keep it neat and blooming. Deep pink flower clusters open in summer above green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for fiery new growth.

directions: Grow in full sun so the red-tipped new growth and gold leaves color richly. Shear after the first bloom to prompt fresh foliage and more flowers. Pink flower heads open over the colorful mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil for a durable compact shrub. Prune in late winter to shape and encourage strong flowering shoots. Broad white to pale pink flower clusters open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for large pink flower heads.

directions: Set in full sun and well-drained soil, watering through the first season. Deadhead spent blooms and prune in late winter to keep it vigorous. Large soft pink flower clusters open in summer above green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a low white-flowered shrub.

directions: Grow in full sun in average soil and water during dry periods. Shear after bloom and cut back in late winter to renew the compact form. Flat white flower clusters open over green foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for golden arching foliage.

directions: Give this shrub room so the golden arching branches can display their form. Prune old canes just after bloom since flowers form on old wood. White flower clusters cascade along the gold-leaved stems in late spring.

planting.note: Set out dormant or leafed-out plants in spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, then backfill and water deeply to settle the roots. Prune out a few of the oldest canes right after the spring flush of rosy-pink trumpets fades to keep the shrub open and vigorous. The main show comes in late spring, with scattered reblooming through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant at the same depth the shrub grew in its container and mulch to hold soil moisture. This species opens its fragrant pink flowers earlier than most weigelas, so hold pruning until the bloom fades. Enjoy the honey-scented flowers in mid- to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring with light afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Give this species a sheltered site with moist, humus-rich soil and dappled light. Unlike its pink cousins, it produces soft sulphur-yellow bells marked with orange throats. Watch for the unusual yellow flowers in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Set this large, vigorous species where it has room to arch outward, and water regularly the first season. Its flowers open near-white and deepen to rose-red as they age, giving a two-toned effect. Blooms cover the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in ground that drains freely and mulch around the base to keep roots cool. The deep rose-pink flowers are held on softly hairy stems and leaves. Expect the flush of bloom in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Site this cultivar in full sun, since shade washes out its dark burgundy foliage. Pair the glossy purple leaves with the rosy-pink spring flowers for strong contrast, and shear lightly after bloom if you want a tidier shape. Peak bloom arrives in late spring with light rebloom later on.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a border or in a container.

directions: Use this low, mounding dwarf as an edging plant or in pots where its dark foliage stays close to the ground. Give it full sun so the leaves hold their deep wine tone through summer. The small pink flowers appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring with some afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant this compact grower where its cream, pink, and green variegated leaves can be seen up close. Protect it from harsh midday sun, which can scorch the pale leaf margins. Pink flowers dot the mound in late spring while the foliage carries the rest of the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact selection keeps deep burgundy leaves on a rounded frame smaller than Wine & Roses. Plant in full sun and give it room to fill in without crowding. Rosy-pink flowers cover the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for best leaf color.

directions: Set this wide, low spreader where its horizontal habit can sprawl gracefully over a slope or wall edge. Full sun keeps the dark purple leaves richly colored. Bright magenta-pink flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this upright cultivar full sun and space to reach its mature size. Its clear red flowers hold their color well and often rebloom in late summer. The main display comes in late spring with a second flush later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this vigorous, tall cultivar as a background shrub or informal hedge. Thin out old wood after flowering to keep it producing on younger stems. Ruby-red trumpets crowd the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border.

directions: This old garden favorite bears deep carmine-red flowers over a long season. Plant in full sun and prune lightly after the first flush to encourage repeat bloom. Flowering begins in late spring and continues sporadically into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Choose a sunny spot to bring out the dark purple-green cast of the foliage. Deep pink to red buds open to pink flowers against the bronzed leaves. The bloom peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this classic where its cream-edged green leaves can brighten a border. Remove any all-green shoots that revert so the variegation stays true. Soft rose-pink flowers appear in late spring above the patterned foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring near the front of a bed.

directions: This compact form carries the same cream-margined leaves on a smaller, denser mound. Use it in small gardens or toward the edge of a mixed planting. Pink flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very cold-hardy dwarf.

directions: This tough, low-growing selection tolerates colder winters than many weigelas. Plant in full sun and leave it largely unpruned to keep its neat rounded form. Ruby-pink flowers with a light fragrance appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; cold hardy.

directions: Part of a hardy compact series, this shrub carries dark-edged green leaves with a purple tint. Plant in full sun and water through dry spells while young. Red flowers with yellow throats open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; very cold hardy.

directions: A dwarf from the hardy Canadian series, this plant pairs bronze-purple foliage with bright flowers. Give it full sun and well-drained soil. Red flowers with pale yellow throats appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; cold hardy.

directions: This durable cultivar blooms over an unusually long stretch of the season. Plant in full sun and remove spent wood after the first heavy flush. Pink flowers with a paler center open in late spring and continue into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This showy selection opens flowers in a mix of red, pink, and white on the same plant. Plant in full sun for the strongest flowering. The multicolored display peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this upright shrub as a backdrop or informal screen. Its flowers open white and blush to soft pink as they mature. Bloom fills the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: One of the few pure-white weigelas, this cultivar keeps its flowers white without fading to pink. Plant in full sun and prune after flowering to shape it. Clean white trumpets cover the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: The foliage of this cultivar shifts from chartreuse to a pale, ghostly ivory-yellow as the season advances. Plant in full sun to bring out the leaf color and set off the flowers. Deep pink to red flowers appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for best variegation.

directions: This selection carries green leaves edged in bright golden-yellow for season-long color. Plant in full sun so the variegation stays vivid. Red flowers stand out against the gold-edged foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring with light shade in hot afternoons.

directions: Grown for its bright golden-yellow leaves, this shrub makes a striking contrast with its red flowers. Give it some afternoon shade to keep the yellow foliage from scorching. The red bloom against gold leaves peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light shade to protect the foliage.

directions: This old cultivar has soft golden-yellow spring foliage that ages to lime green. Plant in dappled light, since full sun can burn the tender leaves. Pink flowers open above the bright leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for repeat bloom.

directions: This reblooming cultivar flowers heavily in spring and keeps producing new blooms into fall without deadheading. Plant in full sun and feed lightly in spring to fuel the long flowering. Bright pink trumpets return in waves from late spring onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: A rebloomer that carries clear red flowers from spring into autumn. Plant in full sun and water evenly to keep the repeat bloom coming. The first heavy flush opens in late spring, followed by later flushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This reblooming selection opens pale pearl-white flowers that pick up a soft pink blush as they age. Plant in full sun for the fullest repeat display. Flowering starts in late spring and recurs through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This heavy bloomer produces white, pink, and red flowers together for a tricolor look. Plant in full sun so the branches flower densely from base to tip. The multicolor show peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Unusual among weigelas, this compact plant opens creamy pale-yellow flowers with darker centers. Plant in full sun for the best flower count. The soft yellow bloom covers the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact selection pairs green foliage with unusually large deep-red flowers. Plant in full sun and give it well-drained soil. The oversized maroon-red blooms open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a bed or in a container.

directions: This true dwarf stays low and tidy, good for edging or pots. Plant in full sun and it needs little pruning to keep its shape. Dark red flowers dot the small mound in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for strong rebloom.

directions: This reblooming cultivar carries bright crimson-red flowers with a small white eye. Plant in full sun and it flowers again through summer without deadheading. The first flush arrives in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact grower has dark burgundy leaves with contrasting yellow-green veins. Plant in full sun to hold the deep foliage color. Magenta-pink flowers open against the dark leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This selection has deep bronze-purple foliage on a moderately sized frame. Plant in full sun to deepen the leaf color. Rosy-pink flowers stand out against the dark leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: An heirloom cultivar bearing large rosy-carmine flowers with a paler throat. Plant in full sun and thin old wood after bloom to keep it vigorous. The generous flowering peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This upright cultivar is prized for its rich purplish-red flowers and good cold hardiness. Plant in full sun and prune out the oldest canes after flowering. Deep red trumpets crowd the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring near the front of a border.

directions: This dwarf forms a dense, rounded mound well suited to small spaces. Plant in full sun and it needs only light shaping. Soft pink flowers cover the compact shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for continuous bloom.

directions: This reblooming selection flowers heavily in spring and keeps going through summer. Plant in full sun and water steadily to sustain the long bloom. Red flowers first open in late spring and recur into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: This species bears pale yellow to greenish-yellow flowers, an uncommon color in the genus. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil with some shade in hot climates. The soft yellow bells open in mid- to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; good cold hardiness.

directions: This vigorous, cold-hardy cultivar forms a broad shrub covered in lilac-pink flowers. Plant in full sun and give it plenty of room to spread. The generous bloom peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the crown level with the surrounding grade and water deeply through the first season. Prune out the oldest stems right after flowering, since blooms form on the previous year's wood. Expect arching sprays of pure white bells in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low edging or groundcover mass.

directions: Space plants closely to knit together as a spreading carpet and keep the roots evenly moist while establishing. Shear lightly after the flush of bloom to maintain a tidy mound. White star-shaped flowers cover the low branches in mid to late spring, and foliage often turns burgundy in fall.

planting.note: Site in spring where the chartreuse foliage catches morning light.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil, giving a little afternoon shade in hot regions to protect the bright yellow leaves from scorch. Thin older canes after bloom to keep the plant open and colorful. Pearl-like buds open to fragrant white flowers against the lime-green foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a low, spreading groundcover mound.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil and keep watered while roots establish, then reduce to occasional deep soaks. A light shearing after flowering keeps the low habit neat. Pink-and-white blossoms cover the compact branches in spring, and the foliage flushes burgundy in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low border or slope cover.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil and mulch to hold moisture around the shallow roots. Prune only lightly and only just after the flowers fade. Clean white star flowers smother the spreading branches in spring, followed by reliable purple fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a tall flowering screen is wanted.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and allow space for the arching, upright form to develop. Thin old wood after bloom to keep flowering vigorous. Double white flowers tinged rosy-pink on the outside open in profusion in early summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny mixed border.

directions: Plant in fertile, free-draining soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Prune out a portion of the oldest stems each year after flowering to prevent congestion. Double flowers open pink-flushed and fade paler in early summer on upright panicles.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the heaviest bloom.

directions: Plant in ordinary garden soil with good drainage and water during dry spells. Remove several of the oldest canes at ground level after the flowers finish. Fully double white flowers backed with faint pink crowd the upright stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny shrub border.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and site in full sun for the strongest flowering. Rejuvenate by removing the oldest stems just after the bloom period. Slender clusters of white flowers appear on arching branches in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its graceful arching form has room.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and shelter from harsh wind. Thin some older wood after flowering to keep the plant airy. Broad clusters of pink-tinged white flowers open on arching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and keep evenly moist the first year. Prune out a few of the oldest stems after bloom to renew the shrub. Rich lilac-pink buds open to pale flowers in generous clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border for the boldest flower color.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil and water through establishment. Cut back a share of the oldest flowered stems each year after bloom. Large rose-pink flowers with darker reverses open in early summer on upright branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in a mixed shrub planting.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil and give it room to reach a broad, upright shape. Thin out the oldest wood right after flowering to keep it blooming well. Large mauve-pink flowers edged white and striped purple on the reverse appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest pink tones.

directions: Plant in well-drained garden soil and keep watered during establishment and drought. Renew by removing a few old stems at the base once flowering ends. Deep rose-pink flowers with paler faces open in dense clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water regularly the first season. Prune lightly after flowering to shape and to encourage strong new wood. Clusters of rounded, double pink pompon-like flowers weigh down the branches in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact flowering shrub for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and mulch to hold moisture. Remove some of the oldest stems after flowering to keep it dense and tidy. Bell-shaped flowers, pink outside and white within, open on arching branches in late spring.
